- A DevOps engineer what exactly broke in production at 3 AM during a “small” deployment

- A Cloud engineer why the AWS bill suddenly doubled overnight

- A Kubernetes engineer why the pod is in CrashLoopBackOff

- A Backend developer why it works locally but not in prod

- A Frontend developer why it only breaks in Safari

- An SRE what happened during the midnight incident

- A Fresher why they’re still “upskilling”

- A Developer when they last slept properly

candidates.text:
The issue affecting Edge Functions has now been fully resolved, and users have reported Edge Functions operating as expected.

The root cause was a denial-of-service event that led to elevated error rates and 504 responses across impacted regions.

Mitigations have been implemented to restore stability.
We will be publishing a full post-mortem with additional details once our internal review is complete.

Sorry for the inconvenience this issue caused, and thank you for your patience while we worked to resolve it.

Stop underpaying DevOps engineers if you don't want to lose millions.

If you do not want your DevOps engineer to:

Slow your deployments down
- Break production at 3 am
- Run your cloud bill through the roof
- Expose customer data to the internet

Then stop making a lowball offer after putting an experienced engineer through 5 rounds of interviews.

You want someone who can:
- Build and maintain CI/CD pipelines that deploy your product dozens of times a day without breaking anything

- Manage Kubernetes clusters running thousands of containers across multiple availability zones, keeping them healthy, secure and cost-efficient

- Write Terraform that provisions your entire cloud infrastructure as code so nothing is ever touched manually and everything is auditable

- Understand networking, Linux internals, container runtimes, cloud security, cost optimization, observability and incident management

And then you post the job at a salary that does not match a single one of those responsibilities.

Remember: The cost of one production outage will exceed the salary gap you were trying to save on.

I said I already learned. That is why I am saying no.

Build an incident response agent with read-write MCP tools for autonomous diagnosis, remediation, and post-mortem documentation - https://t.co/3MKe6Ivvwz

It's 3 AM, your pager goes off, and the API is throwing 500s. You're half-awake, staring at dashboards, correlating metrics and logs across a dozen services while customer impact grows by the minute. This notebook builds an SRE incident response agent that handles that workflow autonomously: investigating incidents, identifying root causes, applying remediations, and documenting the results.

In Notebook 02, we built an observability agent that could read from external systems. This notebook goes further. The agent can also take action, editing configuration files and restarting services to fix the problems it finds. It uses the Claude Agent SDK with MCP tools scoped for safe infrastructure access.

If you still think DevOps in 2026 is just setting up a CI/CD pipeline like we used to in 2015, you're honestly living in the past. Earlier it was mostly build, test, deploy and done. Now it's completely different.

You're expected to understand the cloud bill, security issues, scaling problems, monitoring gaps, why latency suddenly spiked, and what went wrong during the last incident. Its not just pushing code anymore.

You deploy it, you monitor it, and if it breaks, you're on the call. More tools, yes. But more ownership also.

Amazon data center incident in the UAE just reminded everyone of one thing:

A region can fail.

Whether it’s fire, power outage, human error, or something unexpected —
single-region architecture is a risk.

When designing cloud systems, always assume:

👉 A full region outage is possible.

Design principles 👇

1. Multi-AZ is not enough
2. Replicate data across regions
3. Use geo-redundant storage
4. Keep off-site backups
5. Design stateless services
6. Test disaster recovery regularly

High availability handles server failure.
Multi-region handles disaster.

Hope for uptime.
Design for failure.

#CloudArchitecture #AWS #DisasterRecovery #DevOps #Resilience #SRE

AI agents score ~70% on SWE-Bench. On a full DevOps pipeline, the best drops to 23%.

DevOps-Gym is a new benchmark from UC Santa Barbara. It tests agents on what actually happens in production software work: build configuration, monitoring, issue resolution, and test generation, not just patching a file.

The problem SWE-Bench doesn't test: DevOps runs in a chain. Agents apparently hold up fine on individual tasks. String them together and they fall apart.

704 tasks from 30+ real Java and Go repositories.

Their results: Claude Code + Claude Sonnet 4 (best tested setup):
- Build & Configuration: 51.85%
- Issue Resolving: 23.87%
- Monitoring: 20.56%
- Test Generation: 13.87%
- All four stages in sequence: 0%

The authors tracked why agents fail: losing context across steps, not reading logs reliably, and about 37% of build failures requiring domain knowledge the models didn't have.

Nobody finished the full pipeline. That's a useful data point if you're assessing whether current agents can own a real DevOps workflow.
Gemini:  Developers who say "we shipped it in two weeks and it's perfect" are the ones who wake up to a margin call because their Node.js server crashed silently during a flash crash.

Why I use edge functions instead of a traditional server:

I have zero servers.
No Express. No Nginx. No Docker. No AWS bill.
Just 10 Supabase edge functions that handle my app.

- Each one deploys in seconds. 
- Scales to zero when nobody's using it. 
- Costs nothing until it runs.
- A traditional server would be running 24/7 for our 150 users.
- Edge functions run for 200ms when someone taps a button.

That's the difference between burning money and spending it.

I disagree. If the DB is down, the url is reaching your app server, so it’s the responsibility of the app server to distinguish between the two error modes, i.e. url not found and db down. 

A “404” is confusing in this case which could mean the short url was never ever created, or it’s just invalid etc.

A server down means a server down, and your customer needs to know it. If the service sends a 404, the client would be confused aka terrible experience. Think about cases when you are having a partial failure where sometimes the url is returning a 404 but on the next request it works. 

System Design in the real world is less  about technical decisions and more about business context aware technical decisions.

I recently launched ai-deploy-one-click, a no-code tool to deploy an AI chatbot in less than five minutes, using @github and @vercel

I wanted to create a more comprehensive demo around it, showing every step of the way that can take you from zero to production, so here it is👇

https://t.co/uxzyiBxgHZ

It's 2025 and you still haven't deployed an AI chatbot?👀
Well, I have the thing for you✨
You can go from zero to production in less than five minutes with this tool - ai-deploy-one-click: https://t.co/ZHCPN51O4b

👉 You configure your application from the comfort of your terminal
👉 ai-deploy-one-click creates the @github repo, clones it locally and modifies it based on your config
👉 It then connects it to @vercel, pushes your API key as env variable and then proceeds to add all the changes to GitHub
🎉 Deployment kicked off! Go to https://t.co/2KxXIfzgvh and you'll see your chatbot up and running in ~1 min

Don't believe me? Take a look at the demo below and see for yourself! ⬇️

GitHub repo: https://t.co/ZHCPN51O4b
UI Template: https://t.co/tEtvZI46ON

The era of BotOps is here.

On October 20th 2025, your monitoring tools were down. So were your on-call alerts. So were many online classrooms, developer tooling, cybersecurity platforms. Here's what we found when we tracked hundreds of SaaS providers through the AWS outage.

AWS in 2025

- Had 38 outages across services and regions

- Saw its average monthly MTTR go up in Q3 compared to Q1 and Q2

- Saw its us-east-1 region face the highest number of outages

- Triggered outages across thousands of SaaS providers with the Oct 20th outage (~15 hours), with some of them lasting longer than a day.

Read the complete AWS Outage Report 2025 on the IncidentHub blog.

If your application is stable but takes months to deploy a single new feature, it is NOT an asset. ⬇️

✅ Custom Legacy Application Modernization
✅ NextGen DevOps with CI/CD pipelines.
✅ Automation of manual processes.
✅ Scalable re-architecture & API-first design.
✅ Continuous quality engineering

Don't mistake standing still for staying stable.

Hey, that's a 403 Forbidden on https://t.co/d9A8I2SRCk – server is denying access temporarily (message says wait & retry). 

Quick fixes: 
- Wait 10-30 mins & refresh
- Clear browser cache/cookies or try incognito
- Switch networks/device/VPN
- Check if Konami site is down via downdetector

Had a conversation today about the importance of solid monitoring in our DevOps pipeline. It’s easy to overlook until something breaks. Building reliable systems is less about flashy tools and more about consistent practices. Ensure your alerts are useful, not just noise. Trust me, you don’t want to be on call at 3 AM figuring out why your dashboard lit up like a Christmas tree. Remember, it’s about creating a culture where learning from incidents becomes second nature. Keep iterating; there’s always room to improve.

@supabase This is completely unacceptable. Our production applications that rely on Supabase APIs and databases are fully down — real users cannot log in, and our business is taking a direct hit because of this.

Suggesting a VPN or alternate DNS as a "workaround" is not a solution for production-grade infrastructure. Our end users cannot be expected to configure VPNs just to access our apps.

We need answers to the following:
• A clear ETA for full resolution
• Details on what exactly caused this
• Compensation or SLA credits for the downtime

Also, some critical questions once restoration is complete:
• Will login & sign-in functionality automatically resume for our apps, or do we need to make changes on our end?
• Will our existing projects that use Supabase APIs & databases continue to work normally after restoration, or will there be data/config issues we need to fix?

We trusted Supabase as a reliable backend — please act with the urgency this situation demands and give us a clear, transparent update.

@levelsio Always worth adding a CHECK constraint or an upper bound on currency columns. Nobody thinks the Iranian Rial will hit 1,000,000 per dollar until it does. One line in your schema saves a production incident.
@Rhymestyle you mean like server side rollback?

Sequential Cypher pipeline with fail-fast Guardian validators is production-sharp—early abort saves cycles and keeps every mutation atomic. 

xAI runs ordered validation stages on KG writes: sequential checks inside one tx, rollback on first violation. Batching all checks would hide failures and spike latency on bad data.

Sunday deploy ritual: pushed updates to all 6 agents on the Mac mini. Total downtime: 0 seconds.

The stack: OpenClaw gateway, 6 specialized agents (ops, research, code, growth), markdown memory, 14 repos under management.

No Kubernetes. No Docker. No cloud bill. Just a $600 Mac mini and plain text files that agents actually read.

Most "AI infrastructure" is a solution looking for a problem. A good https://t.co/SIctpI6cNC file beats a $50K/yr platform.

The final barrier isn't technical; it's trust. Moving from read-only AI to write-access agents requires a mindset shift in DevOps. Question for you: Would you trust an AI agent with autonomous rollback permissions in production? #Azure #SRE #AI

API exposing: No auth on public APIs means anyone (no login needed) can query your endpoints and pull private user data.

Surprise cloud bill: No rate limits let scrapers/bots hammer your API nonstop, burning through server resources and racking up huge unexpected costs.

3. Open CORS (*): Any website can call your APIs from a browser, silently stealing data via cross-origin requests.

Vibe-code fast, but lock these down before launch!

@elonmusk @AdamLowisz The part nobody talks about with production agents: error recovery. The LLM calls are easy. Building rollback states for when they inevitably go sideways is the actual engineering.

If your AI workflow can’t:

– rollback
– show logs
– limit blast radius

it’s not ready for production.

Stage 4: Deploy.

Automated not manual.

Staging first, then production. Canary deployment — route a small percentage of traffic to the new version, watch metrics, promote or rollback based on data.

The deploy should be boring. If it's exciting, your pipeline needs work.

Thread: Why autonomous AI content loops fail in production (and how to fix them). 1) Weak input ownership causes silent drift. 2) No pre-publish gate means low-quality output ships. 3) Missing rollback turns small errors into long outages. https://t.co/BlUZwbEKBu
